unresolved_same_name.why: split names failed mini-gate (REJECT both. (1) dining_experience_demand — "dining_experience" is a vague/invented phrase, not a concrete thing, actor, or standard financial term (violates R3 word-order anchor of concrete thing->metric, R4 ban on invented synonyms/vague phrases, R7 ban on vague descriptors too broad to name a causal variable). It is also not clearly distinct from the sibling name — casual dining IS a dining experience — so the homonym split stays ambiguous (R10 last bullet: unresolved ambiguity -> reject). The ontology's own example for this domain is restaurant_traffic. (2) casual_dining_industry_demand — "industry" is a redundant scope/category filler that adds no causal content (R7 category-word ban, R9 only include parts the evidence attributes, R8 keep names short); stripping it gives the canonical form casual_dining_demand, the exact name being split, so under the one-name-per-driver canonicalization rule (§2) this proposal collapses back into the original homonym and resolves nothing. Both names pass mechanical format checks (lowercase snake, no tickers, no state/direction/period tokens), but vagueness and canonical-collapse fail the gate, so all_admit=false.) — parked fail-close: True homonym: r1/r2 quote "resilient consumer demand for the... dining experiences we provide our guests" and r3 (own comp sales rising despite a softening environment) name demand for the company's own dining concepts — company scope; r2 explicitly contrasts it against "the broader casual dining industry". r4 ("casual dining demand has had more macro sensitivity versus... QSRs") names demand for the casual-dining industry segment — industry scope. Different object and scope fail the 3-check, so the meanings are split. Both new names are absent from existing_seed_names (near neighbors dining_demand, consumer_dining_demand, experiential_dining_demand, casual_dining_industry_sales/traffic exist, so distinguishing evidence words "experience" and "industry" were added).
